text: Welcome to another video. Today, we're reviewing primary abandonment of the hernia sac in patients with inguinal scleral hernias.

This is a technique that's been described over the course of the last few years, where you incise the peritoneum around the deep ring and address that fenestration at the completion of the case. You dissociate the distal sac and minimize bleeding and tissue trauma related to sac dissection around the cord elements. you can reduce operating room time variability, and it has been associated with some good outcomes from a publication by Morel et al.

The surgeon makes an incision on the cephalad portion of the deep ring in a patient with a large aneurysmortal hernia, marking the area for further dissection and abandonment of the distal sac. A large defect is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon develops a standard flap to facilitate mesh placement, focusing on lateral dissection around the deep ring. An intentional hole is visible, and the surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, proceeding with medial dissection to complete the sac abandonment.

The surgeon grasps the peritoneum and carefully dissects away the cord structures and neurovascular elements. A circumferential incision is made around the deep ring, allowing for the development of a critical view of the myopectineal orifice.

The surgeon dissects around the vas deferens, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to establish a clear view for mesh placement. The vasculature is being parietalized to create an adequate pocket for mesh deployment.

The surgeon identifies the external iliac vein and encounters a small cord lipoma obstructing the view of the iliopubic tract. The lipoma is transected, revealing the iliopubic tract, and the dissection is finalized in preparation for mesh placement and positioning.

The surgeon places a heavyweight, extra-large mesh into the pocket created during dissection for an inguinal hernia repair. The mesh is fixated at Cooper's ligament and along its superior edge. The flap is then closed from lateral to medial.

One thing I will say is that since these fenestrations can be somewhat big, You want to make sure that you don't integrate too much of the flap into your primary closure so that you have redundancy for this portion. Those holes can sometimes look big. They always come together really nice. I've been very happy with this operation.

I do believe that it has reduced variation in operating room time and has been associated with good outcomes None of my patients have had significant seromas or required any procedural interventions. And I've been doing this now for about a year. I'm really happy with the initial outcomes.
